metadata.summary: Converts text chunks into dense vectors for storage in vector databases and semantic retrieval. Covers model selection, batching, caching, normalization, and provider integration.

content.title: Embedding Generation
content.summary: Converts text chunks into dense vectors using an embedding model so they can be stored in a vector database and retrieved by semantic similarity. The indexing step takes {id, text} chunk dicts, batches them, and returns {id, embedding, model, dimensions} dicts. The query step embeds the user query with the same model and measures cosine similarity against the stored vectors.

why.p: Semantic search requires all text to live in a shared vector space where distance reflects meaning. Without consistent embedding (same model, same preprocessing), similarity scores are meaningless — querying with a different model than was used for indexing returns garbage rankings. Batching and caching are required to make the embedding step economical at scale.

list.item: Building the indexing step of any RAG pipeline (chunks to vectors).
Implementing semantic search over a document corpus.
Text clustering, deduplication by semantic similarity, or recommendation systems.
Evaluating embedding model options for a new project before committing to a provider.
Optimizing an existing embedding pipeline for cost, latency, or quality.
Exact-string keyword search is sufficient — embeddings add cost/latency with no precision gain for exact matches.
Very short texts (less than 10 tokens) — BM25 outperforms semantic similarity at this scale.
Corpus is in a low-resource language without a multilingual model — embeddings may produce near-random vectors.
Storage budget is constrained and 1536-3072 dimension vectors would overflow it — benchmark with reduced dimensions first.

section.name: model-selection
section.p: Model selection is the highest-impact decision in embedding generation. Different models define incompatible vector spaces; mixing models within a single collection produces incorrect similarity scores.

rule.statement: Use the same embedding model for indexing and querying; never mix models within a single collection. Pin the model name and provider in a config file before indexing begins; treat any model change as a full re-index event. For OpenAI text-embedding-3 models, prefer native dimension reduction over post-hoc PCA when storage is constrained.
rule.rationale: Each model defines its own vector space. Querying with a different model than was used for indexing produces incorrect similarity scores because the vectors live in incompatible spaces. Config-pinning makes model changes explicit and prevents accidental drift. Native reduction (via the dimensions parameter) preserves the model's internal geometry; PCA applied after the fact discards directions the model considers important.

example.title: OpenAI text-embedding-3 with native dimension reduction
example.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
def get_reduced_embedding(text: str, dimensions: int = 256) -> list[float]: """Use native dimension reduction for storage savings.""" response = client.embeddings.create( input=text, model="text-embedding-3-small", dimensions=dimensions ) return response.data[0].embedding
example.note: Native reduction retains 95% quality at 33% of storage/cost when using 256 dimensions instead of full 1536 dims.

antipattern.description: Switching embedding models mid-pipeline because "the new model ranks higher on MTEB".
antipattern.reason: Vector spaces are incompatible. Switching models invalidates all stored vectors — the entire collection must be re-indexed. This is an O(corpus size) operation that costs time and API budget.

section.name: batching
section.p: Batching is essential for cost and speed. Single-text embedding calls waste API round-trip latency; batching cuts wall-clock time by 10-100x and reduces rate-limit exposure.

rule.statement: Always batch embedding requests; never call the API once per text in a loop. Cap OpenAI embedding batches at 2048 items; validate before sending to avoid a 400 error. Sort OpenAI batch response by item.index before mapping back to inputs.
rule.rationale: API round-trip latency dominates at small batch sizes. The API enforces a hard 2048-item-per-request limit and may return embeddings out of order. Skipping the sort step produces misaligned embeddings for shuffled input lists.

example.title: OpenAI batch embedding with sorting
example.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
def get_embeddings_batch( texts: list[str], model: str = "text-embedding-3-small", batch_size: int = 2048, ) -> list[list[float]]: """Embed texts in batches with correct index mapping.""" texts = [t.replace("\n", " ") for t in texts] all_embeddings = []
for i in range(0, len(texts), batch_size): batch = texts[i : i + batch_size] response = client.embeddings.create(input=batch, model=model) # Critical: sort by index because API may return out of order sorted_data = sorted(response.data, key=lambda x: x.index) all_embeddings.extend([e.embedding for e in sorted_data])
return all_embeddings
example.note: Batching in chunks of 2048 (API limit) and sorting response by index is mandatory for correctness.

antipattern.description: Calling the embedding API once per text in a loop because "it's simpler".
antipattern.reason: API round-trip latency is 0.5–2 seconds per call. Embedding 10,000 texts one-by-one takes hours; batching takes minutes. Batching is not optional.

section.name: caching
section.p: Embeddings are expensive. Caching prevents re-embedding identical texts on re-indexing, which is a major hidden cost in production pipelines.

rule.statement: Cache embeddings keyed by hash(model + text); retrieve from cache before calling the API. Use SHA-256 for cache keys in production; MD5 has non-zero collision probability at billion-document scale.
rule.rationale: Repeated texts (common in RAG pipelines that re-ingest updated documents) waste API budget without caching. MD5 collisions exist in practice. A collision silently returns the wrong embedding for a text.

example.title: Embedding cache with SHA-256 keys
example.note: SHA-256 keys are collision-safe at scale. Cache is checked before any API call.

antipattern.description: Using MD5 for cache keys in production with billions of documents.
antipattern.reason: MD5 collisions exist in practice. A collision silently returns the wrong embedding for a text. The probability becomes measurable at billion-document scale.

section.name: normalization
section.p: Embedding normalization converts cosine similarity (angle between vectors) to a dot product operation, which is faster on most vector databases.

rule.statement: Normalize embeddings to unit length before storing when cosine similarity is the intended metric. Guard against zero-length vectors before normalizing; skip or flag them rather than dividing by zero.
rule.rationale: Pre-normalization reduces query-time computation to a dot product, which is faster than the full cosine formula and numerically identical for unit vectors. Empty strings and whitespace-only texts can produce near-zero vectors that cause division-by-zero or NaN propagation.

example.title: Safe normalization with zero-guard
example.code:
import numpy as np
def normalize_embedding(embedding: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray: """Normalize to unit length, guarding against zero vectors.""" norm = np.linalg.norm(embedding) return embedding if norm == 0 else embedding / norm
def normalize_batch(embeddings: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray: """Batch normalization with zero guard.""" norms = np.linalg.norm(embeddings, axis=1, keepdims=True) norms[norms == 0] = 1 # avoid division by zero on zero vectors return embeddings / norms
example.note: Checks for zero-length vectors and avoids division-by-zero NaN.

antipattern.description: Using Euclidean distance on unnormalized vectors when cosine similarity was intended.
antipattern.reason: Euclidean distance conflates vector magnitude with direction. Cosine similarity requires normalization to measure directional agreement alone. Using the wrong metric silently produces wrong rankings.

section.name: provider-integration
section.p: Different embedding providers have different APIs, rate limits, and strengths. Provider choice depends on privacy, cost, quality, and multilingual support.

rule.statement: For OpenAI, use text-embedding-3-small for cost ($0.02/1M tokens) and text-embedding-3-large for quality. For Cohere, always pass input_type parameter: "search_document" for indexing, "search_query" for query embedding; omitting this degrades retrieval quality by 5-10%. For local embedding (SentenceTransformers), instantiate the model once per process — multiple instances OOM on GPU. For Ollama, use SentenceTransformers instead of Ollama's per-text embedding loop, which is O(n) API calls.
rule.rationale: text-embedding-3-small is the best cost/quality ratio for most RAG systems. Cohere's input_type tunes embeddings for asymmetric retrieval and is a free 5-15% quality boost. SentenceTransformer instances load multi-GB models into RAM; duplicates cause OOM. Ollama's per-call overhead makes single-text embedding 10-50x slower than batched local models.

example.title: OpenAI batch embedding
example.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
def get_embeddings_batch( texts: list[str], model: str = "text-embedding-3-small", batch_size: int = 2048, ) -> list[list[float]]: """Batch embeddings with newline normalization.""" texts = [t.replace("\n", " ") for t in texts] all_embeddings = [] for i in range(0, len(texts), batch_size): batch = texts[i : i + batch_size] response = client.embeddings.create(input=batch, model=model) sorted_data = sorted(response.data, key=lambda x: x.index) all_embeddings.extend([e.embedding for e in sorted_data]) return all_embeddings
example.note: OpenAI's best practice: batch in chunks of 2048, sort by index, replace newlines.

example.title: Cohere with input_type
example.code:
import cohere
co = cohere.Client(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")
def get_cohere_embeddings( texts: list[str], input_type: str = "search_document", # or "search_query" ) -> list[list[float]]: """ input_type tunes embeddings for asymmetric retrieval. Use "search_document" when indexing, "search_query" when embedding queries. """ response = co.embed( texts=texts, model="embed-english-v3.0", input_type=input_type, truncate="END", ) return response.embeddings
example.note: input_type is mandatory for v3 models and improves retrieval quality by 5-10%.

example.title: Local SentenceTransformers (instantiate once)
example.note: Global model instance prevents OOM. normalize_embeddings=True applies unit normalization.

antipattern.description: Using Ollama embeddings in a per-text loop because "it's local".
antipattern.reason: Ollama has no batch endpoint; the loop is O(n) HTTP calls. For corpora of 10,000+ documents, this is impractically slow. Use SentenceTransformers instead for local batch embedding.

section.name: pitfalls
section.p: Common errors that silently degrade retrieval quality or crash at scale.

rule.statement: Pre-check all texts for empty strings, whitespace-only, and token-limit violations before embedding — all three produce zero or truncated vectors. Embedding texts that exceed the model's token limit without truncation loses the tail content. Instantiate SentenceTransformer models once per process; multiple instances load multi-GB models and OOM on limited RAM. Do not instantiate multiple SentenceTransformer model instances in the same process.
rule.rationale: Empty texts produce near-zero vectors that fail normalization. Token truncation is silent in some SDKs but loses tail content. SentenceTransformer instances load multi-GB models into GPU/CPU RAM; two instances of bge-large-en-v1.5 on 16 GB GPU will OOM. Model version changes invalidate stored vectors; agents must version collections and re-embed on deprecation.

example.title: Text validation before embedding
example.code:
def validate_text_for_embedding(text: str, token_limit: int = 8191) -> bool: """Validate text before embedding.""" if not text or not text.strip(): return False # Empty or whitespace-only
# Rough token count (words ≈ tokens) token_count = len(text.split()) if token_count > token_limit: return False # Exceeds model's token limit
return True
# Pre-filter before embedding valid_texts = [t for t in texts if validate_text_for_embedding(t)]
example.note: Validates before API call to prevent wasted embeddings on invalid inputs.

antipattern.description: Embedding texts that exceed the model's token limit without validation.
antipattern.reason: Some SDKs silently truncate overlong inputs; others raise an error. Either way, the tail of the text is lost, producing embeddings that do not represent the full content.

antipattern.description: Initializing multiple SentenceTransformer models in the same process for "parallelism".
antipattern.reason: Each model loads multi-GB of weights into RAM. Two instances of bge-large-en-v1.5 (1.3 GB each) exhaust a 16 GB GPU. Use a single model instance with higher batch_size instead.

template.purpose: Provider-dispatching EmbeddingService with caching, batch support, and similarity computation.
File-based EmbeddingCache with SHA-256-keyed storage for embedding reuse.
